coredata.dc:description: The fabrication of pouch cell prototypes is of particular importance in advancing battery technology by validating materials, concepts, or manufacturing processes. To this aim, these prototypes are generally manually designed at the laboratory scale and therefore subject to human error or reproducibility failures. Identifying these faults before systematic testing or a post-mortem study can greatly improve safety while saving time. Infrared thermography, a safe and contactless technique used in a wide range of scientific and industrial applications, has the potential to become a powerful inspection method in the battery field. This paper shows, through numerical and experimental examples, that pulsed thermography is highly suitable for the non-destructive study of complex systems such as pouch cells by providing detailed mapping of realistic defects in a very short time. Various well-established data processing methods were evaluated in a contrast-to-noise ratio study and visual inspection to provide a set of guidelines adapted for each approach.

Modeled 2D structure. Main colors correspond to the battery unit (white), to the seal (black) and to the terminals (pink and yellow in the upper part of the structure). Misalignment of the electrodes appears around the battery unit in red and blue. Other color codes allow the localization of the different tested defects. Images with the highest number of detected defects for all simulated defects obtained by TSR. Images with the highest number of detected defects for all simulated areas/defects obtained for each tested method and for uniform and non-uniform illuminations. The numbers of detected defects, based on the calculated CNRs, are indicated in brackets.

Description of the dummy prototype pouch cell. a) Sketch of the pouch cell architecture with the types and defects localizations; a) Sketch of the pouch cell before and after sealing; c) Visible images of the defective components of the dummy cell in front and rear face (denoted in relation to the illumination source and infrared camera); d) Visible images of the pouch before and after sealing and coated with a high emissivity graphite paint.

Infrared raw data at times: before illumination, 0.00, 0.31, 0.63, 0.93, 1.25, 1.56, 1.88 and 2.18 s and corresponding thermograms. Digital levels are the internal unit, proportional to the radiative energy, of the infrared camera.

a) and b) Visible images: zones of defective and sounds areas. c) and d) Infrared image from the raw data at t = 0.25 s: Selected defective zones for the CNR study and counterparts sound zones, respectively. Misalignment (in blue), salt agglomeration (in orange), coating delamination (in green) and terminal (in red). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

A) Visible reference: images of the dummy prototype pouch cell before sealing (left); description and location of the main defects in the electrodes (right); B) Images with the highest number of detected defects for each tested method. TCM: images calculated at time 0.15 s; PPT: resulting image at 5 Hz; PCT: third and fourth EOFs; TSR: comparison of the results obtained with the second coefficients for polynomials of order 1 and 4 (p.o. 1 and 4, respectively); C) CT images. From the left to the right: overall view; detail of a crack, missing part, and misalignment; detail of a salt agglomeration. ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fabrication of pouch cell prototypes is of particular importance in advancing battery technology by validating materials, concepts, or manufacturing processes. To this aim, these prototypes are generally manually designed at the laboratory scale and therefore subject to human error or reproducibility failures. Identifying these faults before systematic testing or a post-mortem study can greatly improve safety while saving time. Infrared thermography, a safe and contactless technique used in a wide range of scientific and industrial applications, has the potential to become a powerful inspection method in the battery field. This paper shows, through numerical and experimental examples, that pulsed thermography is highly suitable for the non-destructive study of complex systems such as pouch cells by providing detailed mapping of realistic defects in a very short time. Various well-established data processing methods were evaluated in a contrast-to-noise ratio study and visual inspection to provide a set of guidelines adapted for each approach.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectroscopy

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Within modern societies batteries represent a critical energy storage solution, for both mobile and stationary applications. Improving their key performance indicators (such as energy density, cyclability, charging rate) without sacrificing safety is a challenge involving constant scientific and engineering developments [1]. The design and testing in controlled conditions of cells is a common way of validating existing technologies and evaluating new materials or techniques. This is generally carried out on coin or pouch cells. The latter, which are the focus of this paper, can be composed of more stacked layers (mainly electrodes, separator, electrolyte, and aluminum-coated plastic casing film). These cells can suffer from defects and reproducibility failures during the production process – particularly in the case of prototype pouch cells, which are constructed to represent the final design but are often fabricated using manual operation which are inherent subject to more deviations [2]. These issues encompass layer misalignment, non-uniform electrode coating, gas retention, faulty sealing, or inadequate electrolyte content. Additionally, cycling processes and battery degradation can result in electrode and separator delamination, electrode coating cracks, electrode deformations, exfoliation, migration, transition metal dissolution, electrolyte deterioration, or gas formation [3,4].

Many methods, destructive or non-destructive, can be used to identify these critical defects, as summarized by the work of Waldmann et al. [5] which provides a clear overview of these techniques, their merits, and limitations. Thus, non-destructive methods such as capacity testing, incremental capacity analysis (ICA), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) can offer general insights into the battery's state, providing information on cell aging mechanisms, active material loss, contact loss, and changes in cell chemistry. However, although these electrochemical methodologies are valuable tools, they can only be carried out when the battery is in operation and the phenomena identified are often not precisely locatable. Powerful imaging techniques such as X-ray analysis, X-ray computed tomography (CT), and neutron tomography (NT) allow a precise view of the internal structure of the battery, at the price of high cost, long measurement time (CT and X-ray when several viewing angles are required) or safety risk (NT). Finally, post-mortem analysis can only be performed after cell disassembly. Characterization of a cell's different layers may be achieved through destructive techniques like light microscopy, SEM, TEM, EDX, XPS, ICP-OES, GD-OES, IR, SIMS, NMR, XRD (see Abbreviations section for acronym definition), and chromatography. However, despite the abundance of information obtained through these methods, post-mortem analysis requires rigorous environmental control to avoid injury to the operator and degradation of the various components of the battery under study. In addition, the complexity and destructive nature of the operation can lead to the generation of additional defects. Lastly, the techniques used only provide information on a small scale, which means that tests have to be repeated in different parts of the cell, a time-consuming process.

To address the limitations of both destructive and non-destructive standard methods, an appealing approach would be to employ complementary analysis exploiting non-destructive evaluation methods (NDEs) based on infrared thermography (IRT). Indeed, IRT is safe, contactless, portable, relatively simple to use, suitable for on-line control, and highly versatile: data acquisition up to some kHz (i.e., enabling the study from slow to fast transient phenomena), observation of surface thermal pattern mapping at different space scales (generally from few micrometers to square meters), and for a wide temperature range (generally from −20 to 1500 °C).

IRT is commonly divided into two main streams: passive and active thermography. Passive IRT, a mostly qualitative approach, is based on the detection and analysis of temperature variations on the surface of an object or structure without the application of an external heat source. Defects, irregularities, or potential issues (e.g., cracks, corrosion, water infiltration, or a faulty component) can result in detectable temperature variations on the monitored surfaces. However, it can be limited by its sensitivity to variations in the surrounding conditions, be costly in terms of experiment time, or not offer sufficient contrast depending on the defects sought, or on the properties (particularly optical) of the tested surface. To overcome these limitations active infrared thermography (AIRT), which consists of analyzing the transient thermal behavior of the studied object following an external stimulus, is commonly used. The most encountered excitations are optical, thermo-mechanical/vibrational or based on an induced current and can be characterized by different shapes (pulse, step, square, periodic, or random) and duration. In their work, Ibarra-Castanedo et al. [6] present a very good overview of the different configurations offered by both passive and active IRT.

These IRT approaches have been used in a wide range of applications, such as medicine, agriculture, aerospace, industrial maintenance, civil engineering, etc. [7,8]. However, to date its use within the field of batteries has been relatively unexploited [9,10] and limited to the analysis of single electrodes [11,12], heat distribution during charge/discharge cycles [13–15], thermal monitoring [16], to predict internal heat generation [17], for defects detection by using the self-heat generation during cycling [18], or even to study possible damages in Lead acid batteries in operando [19].

To date, even though non-destructive methodologies exist for internal battery analysis, these only provide general battery information such as aging mechanism, loss of active material, and chemical changes. Alternatively, should the aim be the identification and or internal analysis of the system, disassembling the battery is required. By way of contrast, this provides an internal mapping of the battery without requiring either opening or cycling the battery, offering a fast and safe approach to NDE - with conventional data processing adding simplicity to the methodology.

In summary, the key point is that this study aims, for the first time to the authors' knowledge, to take a further step in the use of IRT in the battery field by using it as a NDE tool for the rapid and easy identification of several types of defects in prototype pouch cells in a way that offers significant advantages over other current analytical approaches.

Nevertheless, despite the potentially attractive nature of IRT for battery NDE, several issues required addressing during the course of this work. One of the main challenges lies in the structure of the pouch cells themselves, which are composed of a set of thin layers with strongly contrasted thermophysical properties (e.g., a coated metallic electrode vs. a polymeric separator) and the wide variety of defects that can occur. These configurations are far more complex than those commonly studied through the application of IRT, such as carbon reinforced plastic composites, metal plates, paint or the wall of a building [20]. To address this challenge, in this study a particular configuration of AIRT, named pulsed thermography (PT) will be applied to prototype pouch cells.

In PT the external stimulus is located on the same side as the infrared camera (reflection mode). This configuration offers several advantages: it is simple to set up, allows samples to be tested with only one side accessible, large surfaces can be explored, experiments are fast, abundant literature is available and, in addition to defect detection, their quantification (thermal properties, size, depth) is possible under certain conditions. Finally, from an industrial perspective, as PT is contactless and relatively compact, it doesn't interfere with production while ensuring personnel safety as it involves no harmful radiation, and produces easily interpretable imaging results, making it user-friendly.

In more detail, a setup of pulsed thermography generally consists of the sample to be studied, an infrared camera placed in front of it, and a system able to generate heat on the same surface with a short pulse of energy (commonly using flash or halogen lamp) [6]. When the surface of the sample absorbs this energy, its temperature rises instantaneously and then, as the thermal wave propagates inside the material, the temperature of the surface decreases. If the illuminated sample is homogeneous (denoted as sound sample or sound zone in the following), thermal waves will propagate through the material without disturbing the decreasing surface temperature field observed by the infrared camera. In contrast, in the presence of heterogeneities (e.g., due to the presence of defects such a delamination or inclusion) the propagation of part of the thermal waves will be affected and the camera will record variations of the superficial temperature field. Thermally resistive defects (or those with a lower effusivity than the sound sample [21]) allow thermal waves to reach the surface of the excitation/observation more quickly than for the sound zones, while a defect that is more thermally capacitive (or effusive) than the sound zone will delay this transfer (see Fig. 1 ).

If the thermal contrast between sound and defective areas is sufficiently great (equivalent to a high signal-to-noise ratio), the user can locate the presence of defects by simple visual inspection. However, this step requires the user to explore the recorded infrared film to identify the frame(s) with the highest thermal contrast for each defect, depending on the depth and thermophysical properties of the defects. Furthermore, given that this is a purely qualitative process, it precludes any further quantification. To address this drawback, different methods have been proposed to automate all or part of the defect detection process, to improve the thermal contrasts recorded on the surface of the sample or to enable a quantification step. In particular, the works of [20,23–25] provide a very good overview of the most established methods, as well as those that are more recent or adapted to specific systems.

In this study, the most conventional and well-established of these methods [23,26,27] (Temperature Contrast Method, Pulse Phase Thermography, Principal Component Thermography and Thermographic Signal Reconstruction, detailed in Section 3) were applied and their merits compared after examination by PT of defectives prototype pouches. These methods were first applied to numerically generated data, and, thanks to a contrast-to-noise ratio study, preliminary guidelines were formulated. Following this first step, methods and corresponding guidelines were applied to an experiment carried out with a dummy pouch cell containing several common and realistic defects.

Numerical simulation of a PT experiment applied to a pouch cell was performed by using a model based on the thermal quadrupoles formalism [28]. The latter was selected due to its ability to simplify complex multilayers modeling and accurately simulate impulse-type illumination [29]. The Stehfest algorithm [30], employing 20 terms, was utilized to perform the inverse Laplace transforms required by the quadrupoles formalism.

In a simple configuration [31], a nonfunctional (i.e. without electrolyte) prototype pouch cell comprises two electrodes coated with two different materials and a separator, all encased within a coffee bag cover (see Fig. 2 ). This multilayer composed of 9 strata is denoted as battery unit in the following. After sealing, a frame of the two layers of coffee bag surrounds the battery unit on its entire perimeter (denoted seal in the following). The uncoated ends of the two electrodes (terminals) are also sealed between the two layers of coffee bag. These three multilayers can be used to model a sound cell.

By adding resistive or capacitive layers at various depths in these three multilayers, or by removing some of them as in the case of electrode misalignment or delamination of their coating, it is possible to model a wide variety of defects.

Thus, for the numerical experiment, a series of 16 1D cartesian multilayers were modeled, each representing a distinct area of a pouch cell:
•
The 9 layers constituting the battery unit, considered as the main sound zone.
•
The seal around the battery unit. This zone is not a defect, but its thermal response is expected to be different from the battery unit and the quality of the sealing must be controlled to avoid any loss of electrolyte or air/humidity intrusion [10].
•
The taps of each electrode sealed in the coffee bag. Once again, these two zones are not intrinsically defects, but as these extremities are easily shiftable during the manufacture of a cell, their detection is of particular interest [2].
•
Additionally, different typical defects were simulated: a misalignment of the electrodes, a small air gap in the seal surrounding the battery unit, local absence of coating on each electrode, delaminations (i.e., layers of air at different depths in the battery unit) and small metallic inclusions surrounded or not by trapped air.

The thermophysical properties of each layer and the comprehensive description of the 16 multilayers are provided in Tables 1 and 2
, respectively.

To emulate a recorded infrared movie, the results of the 16 simulated thermal behaviors can be duplicated and assembled to generate a 3D matrix. Thus, as an infrared movie, this matrix consists of two dimensions corresponding to the number of pixels of each image (length and width, denoted as
N x
and
N y
, respectively) and a third dimension representing the number of frames (or number of time steps, denoted as
N t
). In this way, the temporal evolution of each pixel can be attributed to one of the simulated multilayers. The primary advantage of this approach is that it enables the fast simulation of the temporal thermal evolution of a pseudo-3D structure by neglecting the in-plane diffusion. For a pouch cell, this assumption is reasonable due to the large surface area of the cell compared to its thickness (the Biot number in the thickness lower than the Biot number in-plane) and the short duration of a pulsed thermography experiment. Finally, the computed 3D matrix can be used to directly test different data processing methods, in the same way as for an experimental infrared sequence.

The simulated structure in this study comprises 320 × 240 multilayers, equivalent to 320 × 240 pixels of an image, stemming from the 16 simulated ones. Fig. 3 illustrates this structure, with the numbers indicated in the lateral color bar corresponding to the multilayers described in Table 2.

Non-uniform heating of the sample is a common problem of PT experiments due to the use of flash or halogen lamps [22]. To assess the impact of this experimental issue on the tested processing methods, the simulated thermal behaviors were also multiplied with a Gaussian-shaped window of the same size as the structure. The Gaussian variation of the illumination was arbitrarily centered on the lower right-hand side of the structure, as depicted in Fig. 4 . To further simulate realistic conditions, a normally distributed random noise with an amplitude of 0.1 was applied to the entire multiplier-window.

The thermal behavior of the structure (initial temperature set to 0 °C) was simulated for a duration of 3 s with a uniform or non-uniform energy pulse set at 1000 J/m2. The time step was fixed at 8.3 × 10−3 s (120 Hz). Additionally, a normally distributed random noise with an amplitude of 0.05 °C was incorporated into the obtained results. All simulations and data processing were conducted using GNU Octave 7.3.0 [34] and its “image” package. Fig. 5 and Fig. 6 depict the results at various time points during the numerical experiment, along with the corresponding thermograms for the different pseudo-pixels simulated, under both uniform and nonuniform illumination scenarios.

Visual inspection of the numerical results shows significant contrasts between the battery unit, the seal, most of the simulated defects and, to a lesser extent, the electrode terminals demonstrating the potential of PT for prototype pouch cell NDE as of now. However, as mentioned in introduction, this inspection is delicate because the highest contrasts appear at different times depending on the thermophysical properties of the different multilayers, whether defective or not. Selecting the best images therefore requires the user to explore all the images generated and this approach is only qualitative.

To go further, it is necessary to have recourse to ad-hoc data processing methods. Thus, the next section is devoted to evaluating the most well-established methods for reducing the amount of data to be processed, improving contrast, and potentially paving the way for rapid thermophysical characterization of identified defects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Post-processing techniques applied to a numerical experiment
ce:section.ce:para:
Temperature Contrast Method, Pulse Phase Thermography, Principal Component Thermography and Thermographic Signal Reconstruction were identified as the most well-established techniques to process the data obtained by experimental PT. This section presents an overview of each method and the results obtained with the simulated experiment. It's worth noting that these methods have undergone numerous evolutions and improvements, mainly focusing on the thermophysical characterization of the defects detected. For simplicity and to provide an initial theoretical foundation, these methods were addressed in their original form. To better evaluate their merits and limits, performance of each method, and for each generated defect, was assessed calculating the corresponding contrast-to-noise ratio (CNR, [35]):
(1)
CNR = 20
log 10
DROI mean
−
SROI mean
σ
where
DROI mean
is the arithmetic mean of all the pixels inside a defective region (Table 2, multilayers 2 to 16), and
SROI mean
and σ are the arithmetic mean and the corresponding standard deviation of the pixels inside the sound region (Table 2, multilayers 1), respectively. The sound region is chosen in a zone close to the defective area. Thus, as the terminals (Table 2, multilayers 3 and 4) and the air trapped in the seal (Table 2, multilayer 7) are inserted into the seal, the contrast-to-noise ratio was calculated by using the arithmetic mean and standard deviation from a sound area of the sealing frame (Table 2, multilayer 2).

The CNRs were calculated on 6 × 6 pixels areas for each region and are fully presented in tables S 1-S 8 (in supplementary material). In the following, and for each proposed method, the results presented are those leading to the detection (CNR >0 in this work) of the highest number of defects. When a method leads to the detection of the same number of defects on several cartographies, the one(s) presenting the highest CNRs for most detected defects are preferred.

The temperature contrast ( TC ) method, originally proposed by Maldague [36], is based on the difference between the thermal behavior of a defective region (
T def
) and that of the sound one (
T ND
) :
(2)
TC =
T def
−
T ND
The defect's detectability increases with a higher TC value and is directly related to the defect's thermal properties and depth.

Shallow defects lead to a more rapid appearance of a significant thermal contract on the surface of the sample while limiting in-plane thermal diffusion, further improving their detectability [37]. Thus, the time when the TC reaches its maximum value (absolute value, depending on whether the defect is resistive or capacitive) can be used to determine the time where the detectability is maximum but also, by using more complex modeling and data processing, to estimate its depth [21,38] and size [39].

This first approach was applied to both numerical experiments, with uniform and non-uniform illuminations, and evaluated using the contrast-to-noise ratio mentioned previously. The images shown in Fig. 7 correspond to those with the highest CNRs for all the defects studied. However, it should be noted that some defects can be identified with better contrast at different times (see calculated CNRs in Table S 1 and Table S 5, supplementary material).

The TCM allows the detection of up to 9 defects (8 for the non-uniform illumination case) or zones out of the 15 proposed. Zones with higher CNR values correspond to the seal and air gaps with the highest thicknesses.

This approach is simple to implement, and the data processing is very fast (about 0.6 s on the computer used to process the
N x
×
N y
×
N t
=27 million data). However, TCM is sensitive to the non-uniform illumination which can lead to erroneous results (CNR leading to false positives or negatives) depending on the illumination of the sound zone chosen as a reference compared with that of other zones. In addition, the selection of the image(s) with the highest thermal contrast for each defect can be delicate due to measurement noise, with the thermogram subtraction operation approaching a derivative that leads to a reduction in the CNR of the processed data. This is even more problematic when a thermophysical defect characterization step is required, as this involves additional derivatives of the data obtained [21]. To mitigate these problems, various methods have been proposed, including those presented in the following paragraphs.

The Pulse Phase Thermography method proposed by Maldague and Marinetti [40] is based on the transformation of the thermal behavior of the sample tested by PT from the time domain to the frequency domain to, ideally, study its responses to stimuli of a broad range of frequencies. This approach, therefore, combines the advantages of the pulsed thermography (simple and fast) to those of the Lock-in thermography [41] (higher sensitivity to deeper defects, less sensitivity to experimental parameters like the non-uniformity of the heating source, and the thermophysical properties of the specimen).

The data processing involves the discrete Fourier transform, generally implemented by using the Fast Fourier Transform algorithm, of the temporal temperature decay, pixel by pixel, after the pulsed energy deposition:

F n
, respectively. The latter can finally be used to compute the corresponding amplitude ( A ) and phase ( ϕ ):
(4)

The phase spectrum is generally the most widely used in NDE, as it is less sensitive to experimental conditions while still allowing further quantification of defects [26]. Finally, the choice of the frequency, or frequencies, best suited to identifying defects can be the subject of a separate study [20]. In this work, the frequencies tested were 50, 20, 10, 5, 4, 2, 1, 0.5, 0.25, and 0.1 Hz with the optimal ones determined by calculating the CNR ratio for both numerical examples.
The results obtained show that the lowest frequencies (from 0.1 to 0.5 Hz) allow the detection of a maximum number of defects (7 in this case, see Fig. 8 ).

The defects detected by using the PPT method generally exhibit higher CNRs than when using the TCM. However, some defects detectable using the TCM are no longer detected using the PPT (misalignments). In addition to the increase in CNR, this fast method allows the processing of a reduced number, or even a single frequency, instead of a set of thermograms, while remaining insensitive to the non-uniformity of the illumination.

Principal component thermography, proposed by Rajic [42], is similar to PPT in that the data recorded during a PT experiment are projected onto a set of orthogonal basis functions. However, PPT uses a Fourier transform to project data onto a set of sinusoidal basis functions, which is not optimal as the signals obtained during a PT test decay exponentially and tend to stabilize over long times. Thus, it is proposed to use PCT which consists of a projection onto bases of orthogonal functions obtained by singular value decomposition (SVD). This allows the computation of an ordered set of orthogonal statistical modes (also known as empirical orthogonal functions, EOFs) that provide the strongest basis to project the data [26].

The first step of the PCT is to reorganize the data from the PT experiment from a 3D matrix (2 dimensions for the pixel numbers of each frame and a third dimension for each time step:
N x
×
N y
×
N t
, respectively) to a 2D matrix with dimensions M ×
N t
with M =
N x
N y
. This rectangular matrix, denoted A can be decomposed as follow:
(6)
A =
USV T
where the matrix U ( M ×
N t
) is the set of ordered (the first being the most representative of the data variability) empirical orthogonal functions, S (
N t
×
N t
) is a diagonal matrix containing the ordered singular values of A and
V T
is a transpose of a (
N t
×
N t
) matrix containing the orthogonal functions associated with the characteristic time behavior.

In general, a very small number of singular values are needed to accurately represent the thermal behavior of a PT experiment. Thus, matrices U , S , and
V T
can be truncated to their first R strongest singular values ( R ≪
N t
) and their dimensions become M × R , R × R , and R ×
N t
, respectively. In addition to reducing the amount of data to be stored and processing only the most significant EOFs, this operation tends to remove part of the measurement noise which, because of its limited impact on thermal behavior over time, is truncated at the same time as the lowest-order orthogonal functions [43].
It is worth noting that the SVD can be time-consuming to compute. However, algorithms are available to only compute the first, and strongest, orthogonal functions [44].

From a practical point of view, it is considered that between only 2 [27,42] to 5 [26,45] EOFs are sufficient to provide significant variations to detect the defective areas. As the choice of the number of modes depends strongly on the sample studied and the experimental conditions, the first five orthogonal functions have been evaluated in this work to provide guidelines suitable for NDE of prototype pouch cells. Fig. 9 shows the results with the highest number of detected defects for the two types of illumination, for both the second and third EOFs.

The two EOFs complement each other and most likely defects that are very recognizable in one mode will not be recognizable in the other and vice versa. Examination of both EOFs enabled up to 14 of the 15 zones/defects to be localized, and subsequently the calculated CNRs were shown to be higher than for the two previously tested methods (see Table S 3 and Table S 7, in supplementary material). As expected, this approach is a very powerful tool for defect detection and allows further processing of only two images but at the price of a more expensive calculation time (around 30 and 60 times more than for PPT and TCM, respectively) and increased complexity, if not impossibility, of quantifying defects [20,27]. It should also be noted that only the two first EOFs, in this numerical example, are sensitive to non-uniform illumination.

The principle of the Thermographic Signal Reconstruction (TSR) method, proposed by Shepard [46], is based on a log-scale representation of the exponential temperature decay of each pixel. The obtained log-curves are then processed by using a least squares polynomial fitting:
(7)
ln
T px
t
=
∑
n = 0
N
a n
ln
t
n
where
T px
t
is the temperature decay of a given pixel, and
a n
are the coefficients of the polynomial from 0 to an order N .

This approach considerably reduces the data storage given that the knowledge of the polynomial coefficients alone makes it possible to fully reproduce a complete experiment by calculating the exponential form of Eq. (7). Furthermore, its main advantage is that the polynomial form associated with the induced strong noise reduction allows post-processing of the data by a wide range of methods based on mathematical differentiation to improve the detectability of the defects, or even to allow their quantification [21]. Thus, first and second derivatives of the reconstructed signal generally increase the CNR [44]. Nevertheless, exploitation of the computed derivatives involves the selection of the best time window for each defect, a potentially time-consuming step as the whole thermograms have to be explored [46]. In this work, focused on the evaluation of the quality of defect detection with the lowest number of images to by processed by the user, the TSR was limited to the first part of this method, i.e., the processing and analysis of the few images of polynomial coefficients [47].

While the principle of this method is simple and easy to implement, the degree of the polynomial must be chosen carefully to preserve the thermal behavior of each pixel while removing the high frequency measurement noise. This order depends on the sample and experimental conditions but generally ranges between 7 and 11 [48,49].

The CNRs calculated for both numerical experiments and for polynomial degrees up to 7 are summarized in table S 4 (supplementary material) while the images corresponding to the coefficients of the polynomials offering the best compromise between high CNRs and the number of detected defects is presented in Fig. 10 .

For the simulated prototype pouch cell, the TSR method allows the identification of defects with a low polynomial order for both numerical tests compared to existing studies in literature. Thus, the first coefficients image resulting from a polynomial of order 1, lead to the detection of 11 and 12 defects for uniform and non-uniform illumination, respectively. However, such a low-degree polynomial limits future quantification steps based on successive derivations. Polynomials of degree 4 are therefore preferable in this case with a similar level of defect detection (12 and 10 for uniform and non-uniform illumination, respectively) and enhanced CNRs.

The high level of detachability offered by the TSR is comparable to that of the PCT, but only using a single image (compared with 2 for the PCT). In addition, the quantification of detected defects is simplified by the consequent number of methods available to post-process the data.

However, the choice of the most appropriate polynomial can be very time-consuming: the fitting can take around 15 and 30 times more than for PPT and TCM, respectively, and it has to be carried out for each pixel in an image.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Numerical results and guidelines for experimental tests
ce:section.ce:para: Several general principles can be elucidated concerning the use of the four methods selected to perform the NDE of pouch cell prototypes by pulsed thermography. Firstly, the number of detected areas or defects, as well as the corresponding CNRs, tend to increase with the sophistication of the method (i.e., for the PCT and TSR approaches), but with the price of a higher computing time.

It should also be noted that some of the defects tested, the metal inclusions (defects #11 and #16, see Table 1), are considered as undetectable with the CNR study regardless of the method chosen. Nevertheless, defect #16 (bottom right of the battery unit) is easy to localize with the naked eye thanks to the layer of air surrounding it.

Among the most difficult areas to locate are the terminals of the electrodes; the coating delaminations of the electrodes, especially the furthest from illumination; and the thinnest layer of air (1 μm), but TSR and PCT have proved to be powerful enough methods to accomplish this. Misalignments can be detected with all the tested approaches except PPT at the chosen frequency. Nevertheless, by using a higher frequency (ca. 2 Hz), these defects exhibit sufficient CNRs to be located.
Finally, air inclusions, including in the sealing surrounding the battery unit, are easily detected with all the studied methods. Fig. 11 summarizes these results.

These results have enabled the formulation of a set of guidelines for the processing of experimental data. It is important to note that there are several significant differences between these and those commonly found in the literature, due to the specificity and complexity of the pouch cell prototypes. Numerical experiments with TCM demonstrated short experiments were favorable and therefore data acquisition should be undertaken at relatively high frequency. This first observation can be explained by the low thickness of pouch cells.
For PPT processing, low frequencies in the Fourier space are the most likely to allow the localization of defective areas.

Regarding PCT data processing, it is preferable, as the first EOF is sensitive to the non-uniformity of the illumination, to explore more than two EOFs to benefit from the complementarity of the results obtained. Finally, numerical experiments carried out with the TSR indicate that, contrary to previous studies, a polynomial of low order leads to better detectability. In other words, the results surprisingly show that for the system studied, a non-optimal TSR fitting (polynomial of low order) can lead to images of polynomial coefficients allowing better detectability than those computed with an optimal fitting as required by this method. Nevertheless, it is recommended to use polynomials orders offering the best compromise between the good CNRs of the images of polynomial coefficients and order >3, so as to take advantage of the computation of the logarithmic derivatives and not to limit the possibility of further quantification of the detected defects. Following this comment, numerical experiments carried out lead to opt for a polynomial of degree 4.
These preliminary guidelines were assessed using a dummy pouch cell prototype as detailed in the following section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pouch cell prototype experiment and discussion

ce:section.ce:para: A dummy prototype pouch was assembled to assess the four selected NDE methods and associated guidelines under realistic conditions. This cell is comprised of nine layers, including two coated electrodes and a separator, all encased within a coffee bag cover (see Fig. 12 ). Defective electrodes, previously aged in a functional pouch cell, and a deteriorated separator were used. The electrodes, both positive and negative, exhibited defects such as material agglomerations, cracks, missing parts, and coating delamination. Additionally, the separator showed many signs of wrinkling and bending. These layers were then stacked to create misalignments. The assembly and sealing in the coffee bag were conducted using partially cleaned electrodes (i.e., the salt deposits resulting from the evaporation of the electrolyte have been cleaned, except for a few agglomerations) under argon atmosphere (0 ppm O2 and H2O). The total thickness of the pouch was 0.6 mm.

This dummy prototype pouch cell, therefore, contains a very large number of varied defects, enabling the quick assessment of the possibility of detection with various methods. Thus, while this system is useful for this exploratory study, it nevertheless represents an atypical example.

The experimental set-up comprises of four main components: an external energy source (Flash lamp ELC Pro HD 1000, Elinchrom, set point fixed at 1045 J), a cooled InSb infrared camera (FLIR A6752sc, spectral range 1–5 μm, resolution 640 × 512 pixels), a sample holder, and the dummy prototype pouch cell. The camera was configured for data acquisition at 120 Hz within a calibrated temperature range of −20 °C to 55 °C. To minimize parasitic environmental infrared reflections and enhance the emissivity of the pouch cell, the latter was coated with a high emissivity graphite coating (Kontakt Chemie Graphit 33, CRC Industries Deutschland GmbH). Infrared data were recorded using the FLIR software ResearchIR Max Version 4.40.11.35 and the data were processed using the same program developed for numerical experiments.

In addition, to compare the potential of the PT-based methodologies with a reference non-destructive technique, the dummy prototype pouch cell was imaged by X-Ray computed tomography (CT) [50,51]. A Micro CT RX SOLUTIONS (EasyTOM 230-199 kV) was used, and the sample was tested under a range of 150–190 W.

Fig. 13 depicts the recorded raw data at various time points and the corresponding thermograms for each pixel. The image recorded at a time corresponding to the flash (t = 0 s) shows a sample-centered non-uniform illumination.

To assess the merits of each method, another CNR study was carried out. In a first step, four zones of interest were selected before sealing the dummy cell (see Fig. 14 a and b): a misalignment, an electrode terminal, a delamination of coating and a salt agglomeration. After the PT experiment, the defective areas (11 × 11 pixels) were identified again by comparing the visible images with a selection of raw infrared images (at ca. 0.3 s, see Fig. 13 and Fig. 14 c and d). Finally, their counterparts (four sound zones) were selected in the immediate vicinity of the defective zones to enable CNRs calculations.

A second criterion to assess image quality was also introduced. This is based on quantifying the sharpness (or blurriness) of the images obtained after post-processing. Indeed, well-defined contours, generally penalized by in-plane thermal diffusivity (neglected in the numerical experiments) and the camera's spatial resolution, are essential to facilitate defect detection. This image quality indicator [52] is calculated by finding the maximum value of the frequency spectrum of the computed Fourier transform of the image (size
N x
×
N y
). A threshold is then fixed at one per thousand of this maximum value. The number of pixels in the transformed image whose value is greater than this threshold is noted as N . Finally, the image quality is calculated as follow:
(8)
Image Quality =
N
N x
×
N y

While CNRs and image quality are good quantitative indicators of detectability, both can lead to false positives or negatives. The final choice of results offering the best detectability therefore combined these criteria and their visual comparison with the photos taken before the sealing of the battery unit.
The results are summarized in Fig. 15 and discussed below. The CNRs and calculated image quality indicators are available in Tables S 9-S 12 (supplementary material).

The application of TCM to the recorded thermographic images combined with the two criteria identified the image leading to the best detection of the selected defects after 0.15 s., confirming that particularly short tests are suitable for this type of pouch cell prototype NDE. The resulting image (see Fig. 15 B, TCM) shows that this simple method allows the localization of internal heterogeneities within the prototype pouch cell. However, the high level of blur compared with other approaches (see the corresponding image quality indicator in Table S 9, supplementary material) severely limits its suitability for further processing, such as image processing or defect quantification.

The results obtained by PPT (see Fig. 15B, PPT) show, as in the numerical tests, a significant improvement in the blur level of TCM combined with similar detectability of the selected defective zones. However, while the selected criteria well suited to all other approaches, in the specific case of the PPT they proved unsuitable for automating the identification of the frequency leading to optimum detectability. Thus, they lead to the selection of a frequency of 2 Hz (see Table S 10, supplementary material), whereas their visual inspection leads more naturally to the exploitation of results obtained at 5 Hz (see Fig. 16 ). At this frequency the results are optimal, showing great thermal contrast with adequate resolution and definition. While future investigations might reveal more optimal criteria for the PPT, such work is beyond the scope of this paper. The application of PPT allows the detection of a greater number of heterogeneities than TCM, as the visualization of a higher number of agglomerations or of the electrode delamination.

It may be noted that the frequencies leading to the best defect detectability are higher for the dummy pouch cell test than for the numerical experiments. Indeed, the optimum frequency (or frequencies) are directly related to the depth of a defect and to the apparent diffusivity of the system under test [23]. The many defects (inclusion, delamination, etc.) included in the dummy pouch cell account for a significant variation in its effective thermal properties and, therefore, have a direct impact on the optimum frequency.

As already observed with the numerical tests, PCT is proving to be an extremely powerful method for detecting the many defects present in the test cell. In addition, the level of blur in the images obtained is among the lowest of the various approaches tested (see Table S 11, supplementary material). Here again, the complementarity of the two EOFs with the highest CNRs for each selected defect and image quality indicators (EOF 4 and EOF 5 in this experimental test) led to detailed cartographies of the internal structure of the cell (see Fig. 15B, PCT). It becomes also possible to visualize in greater detail the peripheric sealing structure, the crack in the aluminum electrode on the opposite side of the illumination, or to obtain better detection of misalignment but at the cost of a longer computational time (ca. 5 times the duration of a PPT post processing, 16 s vs. 3 s for this experiment).

This polynomic fitting-based approach combines good detection of the selected defects with high CNRs and good image quality indicators. The conclusions of the numerical tests are again in agreement with the experimental results: a low order polynomial fitting leads to better detectability, the more contrasted results being obtained with an order 4. As with the PCT, defects from both electrodes (such as agglomerations, delamination, or cracks) can be detected with this approach (see Fig. 15 B, TSR). For this experiment, the computing duration of the TSR is higher than those of all other methods (ca. 73 s when 16, 3 and 0.2 s are required for PCT, PPT and TCM, respectively). Nevertheless, one of the major advantages of this approach is that it offers more opportunities to quantify the detected defects, since it preserves the thermal signal while eliminating the measurement noise.

The CT scan carried out shows that this technique offers unrivalled resolution compared with the PT-based techniques, especially for detecting cracks, misalignment, or salt agglomeration (see Fig. 15 C). However, under the test conditions of this work, due to their low density and thickness relative to the metallic surrounding materials [10], it was not possible to detect certain major defects (such as the absence of coating on the electrodes, small salt agglomerations, and air inclusions) or even to be able to distinguish the sealing of the battery unit. In addition, the duration of the test and the processing of the data obtained was considerably greater than that required by PT, regardless of the processing method used for the latter (a few hours for the CT scan versus between 2.5 s and a maximum of 75 s, depending on the applied post processing).

This work compared, through numerical and experimental tests, different PT (Pulsed Thermography)-based data processing methods with the aim of identifying different critical defects in lithium-ion pouch cell prototypes before any cycling and/or a post-mortem study.

Numerical experiments have created a theoretical framework from which conditions for a well-adapted PT experiment could be proposed to take into account the structural and thermophysical complexity of a pouch cell. It has been shown that very short experiments, of the order of a second, are sufficient to obtain relevant information regarding the presence of internal defects when working on a basic cell containing the main 9 layers. By means of a study of CNRs, it has been shown that thermally resistive defects, such as delamination or air inclusions, are easier to detect due to the contrast of their properties with the various cell components. Conversely, capacitive defects, such as electrode terminal location or metal inclusions, are more difficult to detect. It has also been shown that even simple and fast methods, such as TCM or PPT, can provide pertinent information. However, at the cost of higher computation duration, more complex methods such as PCT or TSR are preferable. On the one hand, PCT offers the most comprehensive internal structure mapping, based on the complementarity of several EOFs. TSR, on the other hand, offers a good compromise between precise mapping and the possibility of further characterization of identified defects.

In a second step, the proposed guidelines derived from the numerical experiments were applied to test a non-functional dummy pouch cell (i.e., a cell without electrolyte and containing various realistic defects). The obtained results were in very good agreement with the numerical conclusions. Thus, the four methods tested were capable of localizing a large number of defects within a timescale of 3 s to <1.5 min. Once again, the results obtained with PCT and TSR methods are the most promising.

The results obtained were compared to X-Ray CT scans, which constitute a reference method for non-destructive evaluation of pouch cell prototypes. While CT scans provide incomparable resolution and superior penetration depth, this work reeled that the PT approach offers significant advantages - notably shorter experimental and post-processing durations (mere seconds up to minutes for PT, in comparison to the hours required for CT) as well as, in this instance, the ability to detect specific defects (such as delamination and air inclusions).

This study shows the applicability of pulsed thermography within the battery field, as well as its complementarity with the electrochemical and imaging-based non-destructive methodologies already commonly used in battery analysis. In addition to the short duration of the tests, the fact that this technique does not interfere with the production of cells while not requiring particular safety measures demonstrates it has the potential to be adapted to respond to several NDE issues as presented in the McGovern et al. recent work [10], from the electrodes manufacturing to the cell assembly or to safely prepare a post-mortem analysis.

Future studies should focus on the continuing optimization of the technique, both through further refinement of these methods and through the investigation of alternatives, with a particular emphasis on discovering an approach which provides optimal detection quality and speed. For this, a thorough screening and comparison of the broad range of NDE methods available for thermograms analysis will be necessary to identify and validate the optimal techniques with a high degree of certainty, which will be an important next step. Furthermore, enabling the in-depth characterization of the identified defects, which, while non-trivial given the complexity of the cells, would represent a major contribution to this field and facilitate a rapid advance in NDE battery analysis. Finally, extending this work to both more complex systems (such as tacked pouch cells, cylindrical batteries, etc.) as well as simpler systems which require greater nuance (such as the control of coated electrodes or debris and separator detection during stack) would dramatically expand the utility of this technique, making it well-suited for a very broad range of applications in the battery field. However, this work represents the validation of the proof-of-concept and demonstrates the potential of the technique for rapid contactless NDE battery analysis.
